retrieved_docs.content: Familiarity with prehospital and facility protocols for any known or suspected toxin exposure is imperative. Personal protective equipment (PPE) is important in safely managing the differentiated or undifferentiated poisoned patient suspected of exposure to a toxin(s).

Identify the roles and responsibilities of interprofessional team members caring for patients with FWS venom toxicity based on their expertise and scope of practice, ensuring that each member can contribute meaningfully to improve patient outcomes. retrieved_docs.title: Poison Control In The United States -- Definition/Introduction -- Goals & Mission of Poison Centers
retrieved_docs.content: The objective of poison centers is to reduce morbidity and mortality associated with poisonings.  Poisons can include exposures to medications, supplements, illicit drugs, household, and industrial chemicals, environmental toxins, plants, and venomous insects and animals.

retrieved_docs.title: Botulism Antitoxin -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Poison control and the state health department will help guide the diagnostic workup in cases where the diagnosis of botulism is not clear. Toxicologists can consult at presentation and follow-up numerous times throughout the progression of patient care. Next, the state health department needs to be contacted when botulism is suspected. They assist with mobilizing anti-toxin from the CDC and will investigate potential outbreaks. They may also isolate and test the food source in case of foodborne disease, and if needed, provide help with food recall if a potential commercial source is identified.

retrieved_docs.title: Rodenticide Toxicity -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Consultation with a regional poison control center or medical toxicologist is vitally crucial to the effective diagnosis and management of rodenticide toxicity. Toxicology experts can help determine to which rodenticide(s) the patient was exposed and can assist clinicians in determining whether the patient needs to be admitted to the hospital. Additionally, toxicology experts can assist with determining the most effective treatment(s) for the patient, including the role of gastric decontamination, extracorporeal toxin elimination, and toxin-specific treatments.

Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team, including toxicologists, emergency clinicians, nurses, and pharmacists, to ensure timely and coordinated care. retrieved_docs.title: Comprehensive Review of Bioterrorism -- Issues of Concern -- Investigations and Management
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis is based on identifying the clinical presentation. No specific laboratory tests exist to confirm tetrodotoxin poisoning. Treatment is supportive. Provide ventilatory support and watch out for cardiac arrhythmia. If the patient presents early gastric lavage or activated charcoal can be considered. There is no antidote to date. [69]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2770
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide (oxid uhelnatý) is classified as a gasotransmitter, a small gaseous signaling molecule involved in physiological processes. In addition to its signaling role, carbon monoxide is closely associated with carbon monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a toxic condition that occurs when inhaled concentrations are high enough to impair oxygen delivery and cause cellular hypoxia. Understanding carbon monoxide’s dual nature as both a biologically active gasotransmitter and a potential toxin is important for clinical assessment, prevention of exposure, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 323
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Disorder due to and following poisoning” serves as a broad classification that encompasses both general poisoning (التسمم) and poisoning by non‑medicinal substances. Within this framework, the specific condition of alcohol poisoning (التسمم الكحولي) is recognized as a distinct subtype of poisoning. The subcategory “Poisonings and Drug Effects” is also placed under the umbrella of poisoning, indicating that drug‑related toxic effects are considered part of the overall poisoning spectrum. Clinical observations link poisoning (التسمم) with adrenal syndrome (متلازمة الأدرينالية), highlighting an association that may be relevant for diagnosis and management of patients presenting with toxic exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 298
retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad diagnostic category that encompasses both التسمم (poisoning) and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances as specific subtypes. Within this framework, التسمم is also classified under the category of suicide and intentional self‑inflicted injury, highlighting that poisoning is a recognized method of self‑harm. Moreover, التسمم is associated with تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين (a form of drug‑induced poisoning) and with متلازمة الأدرينالية (adrenal syndrome), indicating that poisoning can co‑occur with or contribute to these particular clinical conditions. This integrated view underscores the interrelationship between general poisoning disorders, specific toxic exposures, self‑inflicted injury, and related systemic syndromes, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

Collectively, this integrated framework interconnects intentional, medication‑related, vaccine‑related, and unspecified psychoactive poisonings with related clinical entities such as adrenal syndrome, opioid intoxication, various organic mental disorders, and post‑poisoning conditions, supporting comprehensive patient assessment, risk identification, and targeted management of poisoning‑related health issues.

The inclusion of protective substances among potentially poisonous agents highlights that any chemical, regardless of intended safety purpose, may become harmful at inappropriate doses or under certain physiological conditions. Recognizing these classifications supports clinicians, toxicologists and public‑health professionals in selecting appropriate drugs, monitoring therapy, promptly identifying toxic exposures, implementing decontamination and treatment strategies, and educating patients about risks associated with both prescription medications and seemingly benign chemicals.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1627
retrieved_docs.content: The toxic effect of an unspecified substance, especially one that is non‑medical in origin (التأثير السام لمادة غير محددة، خاصة غير الطبية من حيث المصدر) is classified as a subcategory of metal poisoning (التسمم بالمعادن). This relationship indicates that exposures to unidentified non‑medical agents can be understood within the broader framework of metal poisoning, which encompasses various toxic effects caused by metals and guides clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.content: Disorder due to and following poisoning is a broad category of medical conditions that arise as a result of toxic exposure, and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances is recognized as a specific type within this category. This relationship indicates that any disorder classified under “Disorder due to and following poisoning” can include cases where the toxic agent is a non‑medicinal substance, helping clinicians to organize and address poisoning‑related health issues more precisely.

Finally, “Poisoning caused by biological substance” and “Poisoning due to chemical substance” are inverse‑ISA relationships to “Tryptophan poisoning,” indicating that tryptophan toxicity can arise from either biological or chemical origins. This integrated hierarchy delineates how diverse toxic agents, their pharmacologic classes, and associated clinical syndromes interrelate within medical classification systems, supporting comprehensive assessment and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 325
retrieved_docs.content: “Disorder due to and following poisoning” is a broad diagnostic category that includes both the general condition “التسمم” (poisoning) and “poisoning by nonmedicinal substances.” Within this overarching group, “التسمم” is linked to several more specific clinical entities. It is associated with “تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين,” a form of drug‑induced poisoning characterized by leukocyte breakdown, and it is also associated with “متلازمة الأدرينالية” (adrenal syndrome), indicating that endocrine disturbances can accompany toxic exposures. Moreover, “التسمم الكحولي” (alcohol poisoning) is recognized as a particular subtype of the broader “التسمم” category, illustrating how alcohol‑related toxicity fits within the general framework of poisoning disorders. Together, these relationships demonstrate that poisoning disorders encompass a spectrum of etiologies—including non‑medicinal agents, specific drug toxicities, endocrine complications, and alcohol exposure—providing a comprehensive perspective for diagnosis, risk assessment, and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1566
retrieved_docs.content: Accidental nefopam poisoning is a toxic condition that results from exposure to the analgesic drug nefopam, with nefopam identified as the causative agent in these poisoning events. This relationship highlights that overdose or inadvertent administration of nefopam can lead to serious adverse effects, underscoring the importance of recognizing nefopam as the responsible agent for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment, and prevention of accidental toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 650
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Disorder due to and following poisoning” includes specific conditions such as التسمم (poisoning) and poisoning by nonmedicinal substances, indicating that both are subtypes of disorders that arise after exposure to toxic agents. Within the broader hierarchy, التسمم is placed under the parent category Poisonings and Drug Effects, linking it to a larger group of toxicological and pharmacological adverse events. Clinical associations of التسمم include تسمم بالأدوية المؤشرة على انحلال الليفين (a drug‑induced poisoning characterized by levin degradation) and متلازمة الأدرينالية (adrenal syndrome), reflecting the potential systemic manifestations of poisoning. Recognizing that various forms of poisoning—whether from non‑medicinal substances or specific drug reactions—belong to a unified disorder framework and may present with related complications such as adrenal dysfunction supports more comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 912
retrieved_docs.content: structure. Poisoning by other medications and drugs classifies the entity late effect of poisoning due to drug, medicinal, or biological substance, and this late‑effect category also has a child relationship to الآثار المتأخرة لإصابة غير محددة, further integrating drug‑related poisoning sequelae into the same generic late‑effect framework. Collectively, these relationships organize both injury‑related and poisoning‑related late effects under a unified taxonomy, facilitating clearer medical coding, diagnosis, and management of patients who present with delayed consequences of diverse traumatic or toxic exposures.
